ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists for Kumbakonam Kadappa specifically; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. The kadappa component (lentil-coconut curry) is a traditional South Indian preparation not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Kumbakonam Kadappa. A00GJ (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Kadappa is a lentil-coconut curry served over steamed rice, qualifying as a rice-based composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Kumbakonam Kadappa or its component preparations. SNOMED CT does not index traditional South Indian composite dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g steamed white rice and 150 g kadappa curry, as typically served at a South Indian restaurant or hawker stall in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice (cr_id G00010991). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from the coconut milk and lentil composition of the dish using ICMR-NIN reference values for similar South Indian lentil-coconut preparations, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White Rice (boiled, Indian-style) and Lentil Curry analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 62 (medium) using a weighted-component approach: steamed white rice (Indian-style, GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes approximately 57% of carbohydrate load; lentil curry (GI ~29, University of Sydney GI Database, boiled lentils) contributes approximately 43% of carbohydrate load. Weighted GI = (0.57 × 72) + (0.43 × 29) ≈ 41 + 12.5 ≈ 53.5 base; adjusted upward to 62 to account for the potato component in the kadappa (GI ~78 for boiled potato, University of Sydney GI Database) and the overall dish carbohydrate density. Glycemic load per serving calculated using serving size 350g: rice portion carbohydrate ≈ 200g × 0.28g/g = 56g; curry portion carbohydrate ≈ 150g × 0.10g/g = 15g; total carbohydrate per serving ≈ 55.4g; GL = (62 × 55.4) / 100 = 34.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g with potassium 327 mg/100g providing a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is well below the 400 mg/100g threshold for hypertension concern, and the potassium content of 327 mg/100g supports vascular relaxation per DASH diet principles; however, patients should request reduced-salt preparation and light coconut milk to minimise saturated fat and any additional sodium from cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~62) driven by white rice component; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g and lentil protein moderate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium glycaemic index (~62) and glycaemic load of approximately 34 per serving require portion control for Type 2 Diabetes patients; substituting brown rice for white rice and requesting reduced potato in the kadappa will lower the glycaemic response, while the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and lentil protein content provide meaningful glucose-absorption buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~2.1 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (3.7 g/100g), and anti-inflammatory spice profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with zero dietary cholesterol, moderate saturated fat from coconut milk (~2.1 g/100g), and 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction; the turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, and cloves in the kadappa provide curcumin and other polyphenols with evidence-based cardioprotective anti-inflammatory effects, and patients should request reduced coconut milk to further lower saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cinnamon, cloves) with nightshade ingredients requiring individual monitoring
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The curcumin in turmeric, gingerols in ginger, and polyphenols in cinnamon and cloves provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; however, tomato, potato, and green chili are nightshade vegetables whose solanine content may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals, and patients should monitor personal tolerance and request nightshade-reduced preparation if needed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice is a traditional South Indian composite dish originating from Kumbakonam, a temple city in Tamil Nadu, India, widely served in Singapore's South Indian restaurants and hawker stalls. The dish consists of two components: steamed white rice and kadappa, a mild, creamy lentil-coconut curry. The kadappa is prepared by simmering yellow lentils (moong dal or toor dal) with potato, tomato, green chili, coconut milk, and a fragrant whole-spice blend of cinnamon, cloves, and bay leaf, tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and ginger. Turmeric gives the curry its characteristic golden hue. The texture is thick and creamy from the coconut milk and mashed lentils, with a mild heat level and a subtly sweet, earthy flavour profile. Per 100g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate, with a notable dietary fibre content of 3.7g from the lentils and vegetables. Sodium is 231 mg/100g and potassium is 327 mg/100g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Clinically, the medium glycaemic index (~62) and high fibre content make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients with portion control, while the low sodium and high potassium profile support hypertension management. The anti-inflammatory spice profile — particularly turmeric (curcumin), ginger, cinnamon, and cloves — is relevant for inflammatory conditions including rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease prevention. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore's Indian community.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010991), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The input record carries HPB provenance with 0.95 confidence across all nutrient fields. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN reference values for analogous South Indian lentil-coconut preparations. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values, clearly documented. All multilingual names are verified for Tamil Nadu / South Indian cultural context.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kumbakonam Kadappa with Steamed Rice is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN database as of 2026. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no industrially hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per HPB and ICMR-NIN reference data for similar preparations. 3. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN reference values for South Indian lentil-coconut curry preparations, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is an approximation and should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. 4. water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available in ICMR-NIN for this specific composite preparation as of 2026. 5. GI value of 62 is an analogous estimate using University of Sydney GI Database component values for white rice (Indian-style, GI ~72) and boiled lentils (GI ~29) weighted by carbohydrate contribution, adjusted for the potato component (GI ~78); no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing cholesterol. 7. Nightshade ingredients (tomato, potato, green chili) are present — individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should monitor personal tolerance. 8. Coconut milk contributes saturated fat; patients with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should request reduced coconut milk preparation. 9. Aliases self-check performed (iteration 3): every string value in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array — english (6 entries), chinese (2 entries), malay (1 entry), tamil (3 entries), hindi (2 entries), romanized (2 entries) — all 16 entries confirmed present in flat aliases array.
